metadata.title: Gatekeeping Fake News Discourses on Mainstream Media Versus Social Media

abstract:
This study analyzes mainstream media (MSM) coverage of fake news discourse and compares it with social networking sites (SNS) users who reference the term "fakenews" in their tweets. The study employs computational methods by analyzing over 8 million tweets and 1,350 news stories using topic modeling. Building on the theory of (networked) gatekeeping and Herman and Chomsky's propaganda model, the results show that SNS users follow networked gatekeeping practices by mostly associating fake news references to the alleged bias of MSM. On the other hand, MSM coverage tends to link fake news to SNS's negative role in spreading misinformation. I argue here that there is a networked flak activity on Twitter which is defined as a collective negative response to MSM in order to discipline it, change its tone and editorial stance, or undermine the public's trust in it. Keywords fake news, mainstream media, social media, networked flak, Twitter

The empirical study of fake news discourse is still under-researched, and this study is expected to fill a gap in literature on this area of research. Previous research on fake news seems to be more focused on the use of automated bots (

paragraphs: "low-quality information" to become viral on social networking site (SNS), often considering it even more credible than mainstream media (MSM; Qiu, Oliveira, Shirazi, Flammini, & Menczer, 2017). Also, a BuzzFeed News analysis showed that "top fake [US] election news stories generated more total engagement on Facebook than top election stories from 19 major news outlets combined" (Silverman, 2016). Further and according to Collins Dictionary, fake news was chosen as the word of the year in 2017 because it "saw an unprecedented usage increase (of) 365 per cent since 2016" (Roy, 2017).

However, there is no unified agreement by scholars on what constitutes fake news. As will be illustrated in this study, the term is used differently on MSM and SNS depending on the platform and the users' intentions. Fake news can be misleading in many ways (Bertolin, 2017, p. 10), and there are different terms that are often associated with it such as misinformation, which is the unintentional dissemination of false information (Jackson, 2017), as well as disinformation, which is the "dissemination of false information with the deliberate intent to deceive or mislead" (European Commission, 2018, p. 5). A review of 34 academic papers on fake news showed that there are six main definitions, including (1) news satire, (2) news parody, (3) fabrication, (4) manipulation, (5) advertising, and ( 6) propaganda (Tandoc, Lim, & Ling, 2017). Allcott and Gentzkow (2017b, p. 5), for example, mention that fake news stories "have no factual basis but are presented as facts," while Benkler, Faris, Roberts, and Zuckerman (2017) think that they contain some "potent mix of verifiable facts . . . , familiar repeated falsehoods, paranoid logic, and consistent political orientation within a mutually-reinforcing network of like-minded sites." A NATO-commissioned report confirmed the above confusion about the meaning of fake news since it encompasses broad types of information such as "honest mistakes made by reporters, partisan political discourse, . . . the use of click bait headlines, . . . foreign states or domestic groups that would try to undermine the political process . . . through the use of various forms of malicious fabrications, infiltration of grassroots groups, and automated amplification techniques." (Bertolin, 2017, p. 10) In brief, there are various definitions of fake news, a polarizing term that has become highly politicized and weaponized in recent times by different political figures, and this research investigates the nature of discourses surrounding fake news on MSM and SNS to further understand its meaning. This article is one of the first empirical studies that compares between the coverage of fake news in mainstream news outlets and the discourse on fake news on social media. It offers an analysis of a unique data set, constituting of over 8 million tweets and about 1,350 news stories collected from different media outlets that reference fake news.

sections.paper_id: Al-Rawi - 2019 - Gatekeeping Fake News Discourses on Mainstream Media Versus Social Media
paragraphs: Fake news is nearly as old as human civilization (McNair, 2017) and is especially popular during times of conflict such as the period that preceded the invasion of Iraq (O'Shaughnessy, 2004). Social media, in particular, has enhanced the spread of fake news due to integral shareability and connectivity features, yet these efforts have the potential of undermining democracy.

In their propaganda model, Herman and Chomsky (1988) referred to five filters that shape news media including flak which is generally defined as the "negative responses to a media statement or program. It may take the form of letters, telegrams, phone calls, petitions, lawsuits, speeches and bills before Congress, and other modes of complaint, threat, and punitive action. It may be organized centrally or locally, or it may consist of the entirely independent actions of individuals. " (p. 26) The main goal behind using flak is related to "disciplining the media" (1988, p. 2), and the authors caution that it can cost media outlets considerable financial losses especially if "produced on a large scale, or by individuals or groups with substantial resources" (Herman & Chomsky, 1988, p. 2). This propaganda model will be expanded in order to connect it to the study's findings.

Since this study deals with the discourse on fake news on MSM and SNS, it is also relevant to discuss the theoretical concept of (networked) gatekeeping. Inspired by the work of Lewin (1947), White (1950) was one of the first scholars who referred to the concept of gatekeeping in the context of journalism and communication. He stated that the traveling of a news item through certain communication channels was dependent on the fact that certain areas within the channels functioned as "gates" . . . .[These] gate sections are governed either by impartial rules or by "gate keepers," and in the latter case an individual or group is "in power" for making the decision between "in" and "out." (1950, p. 383) White (1950) reveals that the gatekeeping discourse is often "subjective" since it is "based on the 'gate keeper's' own set of experiences, attitudes and expectations" (p. 390). Similarly, Barzilai-Nahon (2009, p. 1) defines gatekeeping as "the process of controlling information as it moves through a gate or filter . . . and is associated with exercising different types of power (e.g., selecting news, enforcing the status quo in parliamentary committees, mediating between professional and ethnic groups, brokering expert information)."

In the above two definitions, there seems to be an information filtering process as Shoemaker (1991) asserts that gatekeeping is more related to cutting down billions of messages into a few hundred ones that reach us every single day. Journalists follow certain standardized and centralized rules in gatekeeping in order to make sense of the world and provide an overview of "important" events that they believe their readers seek and need. This, indeed, partly determines the news selection process and the way traditional news organizations are shaped.

In relation to social media, there is a clear noncentralization in the way users post and share information. However, there are always influentials or popular figures that often dominate the online chatter. Barzilai-Nahon (2008) explains in her theory of networked gatekeeping the importance of vertical communication flow, and Shaw (2012) mentions the centralized gatekeeping activity as most but not all of the online chatter is actually filtered by gatekeepers whose communication flows from top to bottom (Castells, 2013, p. 71). In this regard, the new version of the two-step flow theory of communication by Katz and Lazarsfeld (1955) seems relevant here. The original theory focuses on how "ideas often flow from radio and print to opinion leaders and from these to the less active sections of the population" (Katz, 1957, p. 61). Katz and Lazarsfeld (1955, p. 3) originally defined opinion leaders as "the individuals who were likely to influence other persons in their immediate environment," and this definition remains in use, more or less unchanged (Grewal, Mehta, & Kardes, 2000, p. 236). In the age of social media, some studies discussed that this theory is still valid in the sense that opinion leaders can generate interest and communication flows among ordinary people (Hilbert, Va ´squez, Halpern, Valenzuela, & Arriagada, 2016). For example, Choi (2015) relied on Katz and Lazarsfeld's theory to explain the influence of opinion makers in South Korea who are more effective than online content creators. Also, Southgate, Westoby, and Page (2010) found that celebrity status or off-line fame plays a crucial role in making content such as YouTube videos viral. Several other studies confirmed that celebrities have a clear impact on making some online media content popular (Nahon & Hemsley, 2013, p. 78;Wu & Wang, 2011). In social network analysis, some scholars believe that examining the so-called focal structure is important as it is concerned with the influential group of individuals in a network. The focal structure comprises people who act together in order to form an added power in an online network (S ¸en, Wigand, Agarwal, Tokdemir, & Kasprzyk, 2016).

To sum up, MSM often follows clear gatekeeping processes that determine the way news is made, including discourse on fake news. This is based on a vertical type of communication flow. On the other hand, social media users also practice what is known as networked gatekeeping activity which is often influenced by influentials and is part of bottom-up communication flow (see also Bro & Wallberg, 2014). This study attempts to answer the following research question:
Research Question 1: What are the topics that are associated with the (networked) gatekeeping discourse on fake news on MSM and SNS?

sections.paper_id: Al-Rawi - 2019 - Gatekeeping Fake News Discourses on Mainstream Media Versus Social Media
paragraphs: Two sets of large data were collected. The first set consisted of 8,116,792 tweets which referenced "#fakenews" was retrieved from Twitter for a period of over 7 months (from January to August 2017) using Boston University Twitter platform called Boston University-Twitter Collection and Analysis Toolkit (Borra & Rieder, 2014;Groshek, 2014). This online platform, which is built based on the Digital Methods Initiative Twitter Capture and Analysis Toolset, uses the "gardenhose" Twitter access which offers a small percentage of the available tweets unlike "firehose" option that provides full data access including historical tweets. Only public tweets have been collected in this study, and the tweets were posted by 1,501,976 distinct users. Twitter was used in this study because it is a preferred platform for political discussions especially by famous figures and celebrities (Parmelee & Bichard, 2011), and it is easier to collect public messages from it due to its relatively flexible Application Programming Interface rules (Boyd & Crawford, 2012).

The second set of data was retrieved from MSM using a subscribed journalism database called ProQuest-Newstream. All the news articles that referred to the term "fake news" were collected from 23 U.S. and British newspapers. The original search yielded a total of 1,599 newspaper items including news stories, features, and editorials; however, duplicates were removed and only fulltext news articles were chosen in the study, and the total number of news items were 1,353 (794 U.S. and 559 U.K. news items; Table 1). The reason behind choosing all the available news stories within this time frame is that they are limited in number, and the majority of news items were mostly published in the years 2016 and 2017 due to the increasing popularity of this topic following Brexit and the 2016 U.S. election (Figure 1). Besides, it is important to identify the dominant topics in MSM coverage of fake news, so the selection of this time frame sounds appropriate.

In relation to this study, topic modeling was employed which is a text mining tool that identifies topics in an unstructured corpus. Since there is a large data set retrieved from Twitter, this method was used, for "the application of natural language processing tasks commonly used with Twitter data" often includes "language identification, topic modelling, and sentiment analysis" (Brown & Soto-Corominas, 2017, p. 136). Built on a statistical model and using a specific algorithm, it is part of machine learning based on calculating the most frequent words used and their associations with other terms and phrases.

Topic modeling often functions in two ways: the first produces "each word on the basis of some number of preceding words or word classes," while the second one "generate[s] words based on latent topic variables inferred from word correlations independent of the order in which the words appear" (Wallach, 2006, p. 977). In the study that employs factor analysis (FA), the latent semantic concepts are identified in the examined data. Many research studies employed topic modeling using different procedures and types of software such as the study of large data sets on Twitter (Conover, Ferrara, Menczer, & Flammini, 2013;Ghosh & Guha, 2013), especially that this online platform has become an important venue for news and political opinions (Dyagilev & Yom-Tov, 2014;Vergeer, 2015), while other studies use and/or reference topic modeling in public comments, forums, and journalism-related issues (Berendt, 2011;Boumans & Trilling, 2016;Jacobi, van Atteveldt, & Welbers, 2016;Kigerl, 2017), especially in examining some case studies in media coverage (DiMaggio, Nag, & Blei, 2013;Kang, Song & Jho, 2013;Levy & Franklin, 2014).

In this study, QDA Miner 5-Word Stat 7 is used which offers a new topic modeling tool using FA (Pe ´ladeau & Davoodi, 2018). FA has been employed since the early 1960s in classifying documents and retrieving topics in different disciplines like health, psychology, and linguistics (Borko & Bernick, 1963;Harway & Iker, 1964;Iker & Harway, 1965). In FA, the formula followed involves the reduction of data dimensionality in order to identify the latent content by accounting for each word in the vocabulary, all the words in the corpus, and the common factors or topics. The formula aims at showing the "factor loading" which indicates "the strength of the relationship of each word to each topic," while the data "dimensionality reduction in FA is based on the idea that each word is the representation of a linear combination of underlying hidden variables" (i.e., topics; Pe ´ladeau & Davoodi, 2018, p. 616).

Two sets of large data were collected. The first set consisted of 8,116,792 tweets which referenced "#fakenews" was retrieved from Twitter for a period of over 7 months (from January to August 2017) using Boston University Twitter platform called Boston University-Twitter Collection and Analysis Toolkit (Borra & Rieder, 2014;Groshek, 2014). This online platform, which is built based on the Digital Methods Initiative Twitter Capture and Analysis Toolset, uses the "gardenhose" Twitter access which offers a small percentage of the available tweets unlike "firehose" option that provides full data access including historical tweets. Only public tweets have been collected in this study, and the tweets were posted by 1,501,976 distinct users. Twitter was used in this study because it is a preferred platform for political discussions especially by famous figures and celebrities (Parmelee & Bichard, 2011), and it is easier to collect public messages from it due to its relatively flexible Application Programming Interface rules (Boyd & Crawford, 2012).

The second set of data was retrieved from MSM using a subscribed journalism database called ProQuest-Newstream. All the news articles that referred to the term "fake news" were collected from 23 U.S. and British newspapers. The original search yielded a total of 1,599 newspaper items including news stories, features, and editorials; however, duplicates were removed and only fulltext news articles were chosen in the study, and the total number of news items were 1,353 (794 U.S. and 559 U.K. news items; Table 1). The reason behind choosing all the available news stories within this time frame is that they are limited in number, and the majority of news items were mostly published in the years 2016 and 2017 due to the increasing popularity of this topic following Brexit and the 2016 U.S. election (Figure 1). Besides, it is important to identify the dominant topics in MSM coverage of fake news, so the selection of this time frame sounds appropriate.

In relation to this study, topic modeling was employed which is a text mining tool that identifies topics in an unstructured corpus. Since there is a large data set retrieved from Twitter, this method was used, for "the application of natural language processing tasks commonly used with Twitter data" often includes "language identification, topic modelling, and sentiment analysis" (Brown & Soto-Corominas, 2017, p. 136). Built on a statistical model and using a specific algorithm, it is part of machine learning based on calculating the most frequent words used and their associations with other terms and phrases.

Topic modeling often functions in two ways: the first produces "each word on the basis of some number of preceding words or word classes," while the second one "generate[s] words based on latent topic variables inferred from word correlations independent of the order in which the words appear" (Wallach, 2006, p. 977). In the study that employs factor analysis (FA), the latent semantic concepts are identified in the examined data. As FA measures the variability of observed and unobserved variables or factors, the Eigenvalues is important here because it calculates the variation of each factor in the overall data, with >1 value rule following Kaiser's (1960) criteria. It is important to mention here that there still needs to be contextualization and interpretation to be undertaken by the researcher. Finally, the software provides accurate statistical measures to further investigate each topic with the use of proximity plot and its Jaccard's coefficient that assesses the connections among various words and phrases. The coefficient ranges between 0.0 for no cooccurrence and 1.0 for complete co-occurrence (Tan, Steinbach, & Kumar, 2006).

sections.paper_id: Al-Rawi - 2019 - Gatekeeping Fake News Discourses on Mainstream Media Versus Social Media
paragraphs: This article investigates MSM coverage of fake news and compares it to #fakenews discourses on Twitter in order to understand the differences between the two platforms. The findings of the study show that the top five most dominant topics used in MSM are as follows: "advertising/ads" (Eigenvalue < 5.10), "intelligence" (Eigenvalue < 2.95), "Jon Stewart Show" (Eigenvalue < 2.19), "Hillary Clinton" (Eigenvalue < 2.11), and "Donald Trump" (Eigenvalue < 1.92; Table 2). Due to the lack of space, only a selection of topics will be discussed below.

In this regard, Table 3 provides a list of the top 100 words used in MSM which assists in understanding some of the prominent topics. For example, the most recurrent topic is related to advertising/ads which is firstly connected to "sites" (J < .104) and "Google" (J < .093). In other words, the topic of "Sites for Google advertising" is the most recurrent one that has been tackled by MSM mostly in connection to the financial profits of fake news stories. Upon examining the most recurrent phrases that are made up of three words in the corpus, we find that the phrase "fake news sites" (n ¼ 183) is, in fact, the most recurrent one followed by "fake news stories" (n ¼ 148), "Comet Ping Pong" (n ¼ 101), and "Facebook and Google" (n ¼ 96; see Figure 2). The second recurrent topic is related to intelligence which is first associated with "Russian" (J < .116), "officials" (J < .086), and "Russia" (J < .078). As for the third topic, it is "Jon Stewart" which is a reference to The Daily Show program. We found that the word "real" is strongly associated with Stewart (J < .026). In fact, the second most associated term with "real news" is Jon Stewart (J < .025) and the "Daily Show" (J < 0.024). Further, the fourth topic is Hillary Clinton which is closely linked to other terms like "campaign," "Trump," and "election." However, this topic is also used in reference to "conspiracy" stories (J < .056 with "Hillary" and J < .065 with "Clinton") such as the Pizzagate fake story that involved an alleged child sex ring. In fact, Hillary Clinton is firstly connected to "Conspiracy theories" (J < .034) and Comet Ping Pong coming in the fifth rank (J < .024; see also Table 4).

In relation to Donald Trump, we find that it is associated with other common words like "President," "Mr.," "news" but also "fake" (J < .103) and Russia (J < .070). The most recurrent phrase used in MSM consisting of four words is "President Elect Trump" (n ¼ 75) followed by "spread of fake news" (n ¼ 69), "President of the United" (n ¼ 46), and "proliferation of fake news" (n ¼ 40). It is important to mention a few more prominent topics like "Mark Zuckerberg." In this regard, we find that it is associated with other familiar terms like "CEO," "Facebook," "Chief," "company" but also "misinformation" (J < .061), "problem" (J < .048), and fake (J < .036). In relation to Facebook, we find that the first associated word is news (J < .177) followed by fake (J < .175). Interestingly, when investigating the "social networks" topic, we find that the third most connected term is also "false news" (J < .035), while the topic of "hate speech" is firstly connected to it (J < .034).

As for the data analysis of Twitter, the most dominant topics are "Pleased extremely" (Eigenvalue < 4.81), "Producer admits" (Eigenvalue < 3.53), "Beat social" (Eigenvalue < 2.76), "House Opinion" (Eigenvalue < 2.52), and "changing thinking" (Eigenvalue < 2.38; Table 5). In order to better understand the reasons behind the prominence of the above topics, we need to investigate the most frequent phrases consisting of (4) words that are found in the corpus with the removal of duplicates. We find that "fakenews and garbage journalism" comes first (n ¼ 39,923) followed by "report the great economic" (n ¼ 22,082) and "fakenews CNN producer admits" (n ¼ 19,692). The prominence of these recurrent phrases will be discussed below.

In order to further examine the topics, we need to investigate the proximity plots of other terms. For example, the examination of the most frequent phrases shows that the topic of "fake news" or "fakenews" (topic no. 13) comes in the first three places in different formats with a total of 1,345,014 frequencies (Table 6). This topic is directly followed by "Fakenews CNN" (n ¼ 136,599), which is linked to changing thinking that is part of Trump's tweet cited below. By closely investigating the term "CNN," we find that it is actually the most frequent word used on Twitter (n ¼ 1,690,723) followed by Trump (n ¼ 1,300,202), "Realdonaldtrump" (n ¼ 714,542), "media" (n ¼ 686,885), and news (n ¼ 580,203; Table 7). Figure 3 provides a visual representation of these dominant words and their links to other ones. In this regard, it is relevant to examine the proximity plot of CNN especially that it is the most discussed and referenced topic in the examined data set. We can see that it is mostly connected with Trump (J < .094) but other terms include fake (J < .044), "CNNBlackmail" (J < .033), "exposed" (J < .030), "garbage" (J < .030), and "lies" (J < .020).

We also compared two opposite terms fake and real news in order to examine the differences between them. The proximity plot of the two topics indicates that Twitter users are far more likely to associate CNN with fake news (J < .044) rather than real news (J < .018). Aside from their strong connection to news, we can see that Trump (J < .037) and "RealDonladTrump" (J < .034) are more associated with fake (news). On the other hand, the word "people" (American) is firstly connected to real news (J < .027) followed by a reference to Trump's aid, "Jamesokeefeiii" (J < .023), and "report" (J < .022).

Finally, it is relevant to examine other prominent topics such as "propaganda" which is more likely to be associated with "liberals" (J < .031), "MSM" (J < .018), "Left" (J < .018), and CNN (J < .014), while other terms manifest weaker connections to propaganda such as "Foxnews" (J < .011) and Russian (J < .011).

The findings of the study show that the top five most dominant topics used in MSM are as follows: "advertising/ads" (Eigenvalue < 5.10), "intelligence" (Eigenvalue < 2.95), "Jon Stewart Show" (Eigenvalue < 2.19), "Hillary Clinton" (Eigenvalue < 2.11), and "Donald Trump" (Eigenvalue < 1.92; Table 2). sections.paper_id: Al-Rawi - 2019 - Gatekeeping Fake News Discourses on Mainstream Media Versus Social Media
paragraphs: To answer the research question, the findings presented above indicate that there is an obvious disparity in the way fake news is presented and discussed on MSM and SNS due to the differences in the (networked) gatekeeping practices. As mentioned above, gatekeeping is an information filtering process, for elites and nonelites decide which messages should be edited, filtered, blocked, or disseminated. Due to its news filtering activity that is connected to the gatekeeping discourse, MSM coverage of fake news is more focused on highlighting its connection with Internet sites generating revenues from Google advertising, social media especially Facebook, and Donald Trump. The term fake news sites itself is more related to spreading conspiracy theories like the Pizzagate story. This is clearly evident in the most dominant topics that are identified as well as their connections to other terms. For example, fake news is highly linked to social media and Donald Trump's presidential campaign, while Mark Zuckerberg is more likely to be mentioned in connection to spreading fake news and/or misinformation. Also, real news is connected to Jon Stewart and his Daily Show which became popular long before the U.S. election due to the nature of political commentaries made by Jon Stewart on fake news. The show itself was called "the Real Fake News" (Kothe, 2007). As stated earlier, one of the main definitions of fake news is related to news satire ad parody (Tandoc et al., 2017), which explains the meaning of fake news here. Further, MSM highlights another significant topic which is the alleged relation between Russian intelligence and U.S. officials especially those linked to President Elect Donald Trump's team. It is important to mention here that the news outlets selected in this study are mostly quality newspapers that have often been directly criticized by Donald Trump himself. In fact, Trump regards most of the selected outlets as fake news organizations especially The New York Times and the Washington Post largely due to the way they critically cover his administration and policies (Boland, 2017). More recently, fake news has become defined by "those on the [political] right to mean anything they don't agree with" (Jamieson, 2017). In fact, Donald Trump and his previous aides like Bannon and Spicer repeatedly referred to CNN and sometimes other outlets like the New York Times and the BBC as "fake news organizations" that produce "garbage journalism" (Davis & Grynbaum, 2017;Gambno, 2017). In brief, MSM outlets have focused on covering President Elect Donald Trump and linking him to fake news and Russia as part of their gatekeeping discourses, while the coverage on social media has been mostly linked to the spread of fake news stories, conspiracy theories, misinformation, and hate speech. On the other hand, Jon Stewart and his Daily Show are mostly associated with real news because of the other name of the program. On the other hand, fake news is presented on social media from a different angle due to the centralized gatekeeping discourse by certain influentials or popular online users. The majority of Twitter users seem to reference or discuss CNN by negatively presenting it as a fake news organization. On MSM, references to CNN are very rare, and any related topic is not even prominent in the coverage, whereas many negative terms are strongly associated with CNN on Twitter like CNNBlackmail, garbage journalism, exposed, propaganda, enemy, and lies. The main reason behind this kind of negative attitude against CNN seems to be related to the number of times certain anti-CNN posts are retweeted. In fact, the most dominant topics and recurrent phrases in the corpus are actually fragments taken from larger statements that have become popular on Twitter, and they are all parts of retweets of famous figures especially that belong to Donald Trump. For instance, the most retweeted post was written by Trump on July 1, 2017: "I am extremely pleased to see that @CNN has finally been exposed as #FakeNews and garbage journalism. It's about time!" (n ¼ 39,955). This is followed by another tweet from Trump posted on the same day: "I am thinking about changing the name #FakeNews CNN to #FraudNewsCNN!" (n ¼ 30,708). The third most retweeted post is also by Trump that was written on June 11, 2017: "The #FakeNews MSM doesn't report the great economic news since Election Day" (n ¼ 22,228). As for the fourth most retweeted posted, it was written on June 26, 2017, by the conspiracy theorist, James O'Keefe, who has close ties with Brietbart news, stating: "#FakeNews CNN Producer admits the CNN Russia narrative is 'bullshit.' #AmericanPravda" (n ¼ 21,297). Another popular tweet by Trump reads as follows: "The FAKE NEWS media (failing @nytimes, @NBCNews, @ABC, @CBS, @CNN) is not my enemy, it is the enemy of the American People!" (n ¼ 17,836). Indeed, the most dominant topics on Twitter are mostly derived from Trump and his supporter which provides a clear indication on the way partisan political views become prominent which is also related to centralized gatekeeping activity as the views and opinions of a few popular Twitter users largely shape the discussion on this platform. In brief, Twitter users are more likely to associate fake news with CNN that is negatively linked to conspiracy, lies, and propaganda, while real news is more connected to Trump's supporter James O'Keefe. As part of its centralized networked gatekeeping activity, some influentials especially Trump and a number of his superfans are more likely to dominate the Twittersphere discussion.

In relation to the topic of fake news, it is strongly connected to Trump and RealDonladTrump because of the number of times the U.S. president tweets about this topic. On the other hand, the discussion of real news on Twitter is mostly linked to Trump's aid, James O'Keefe, suggesting that he is a credible and real news source. This is in contrast to fake news coverage on MSM, wherein Jon Stewart and his Daily Show are strongly linked to real news in the sense of satire because of the alternative parodic title of his show. Other topics investigated on Twitter such as propaganda show a clear partisan stance as the majority of associated terms are linked to the political left and/or liberals. As stated above, among the main definitions of fake news are manipulation and propaganda (Tandoc et al., 2017) which seem to fit well into the networked gatekeeping discourses of fake news on social media.

It seems that there is an obvious ideological divide that separates MSM from SNS in relation to fake news due to the different gatekeeping discourses, for the former has emphasized the role of Russia and its connection to Trump, yet this topic is not prominent on Twitter as Trump and his supporters have dominated the discourse. Further, MSM has highlighted the negative influence of fake news sites and their connection to Google advertising, SNS especially Facebook and its CEO, topics that are again largely missing on Twitter. Instead, the majority of Twitter users have focused their attention on attacking CNN by retweeting posts written by Trump and his popular supporters. On MSM, real news is closely linked to Jon Stewart who is not prominent on Twitter, while SNS mostly linked it to one of Trump's superfans, which shows a partisan rift between the two platforms. In brief, the gatekeeping discourses of MSM outlets show an unfavorable coverage of Trump, fake news sites, Google, and Facebook, while Trump and his supporters are highly critical of MSM especially CNN. The networked and centralized gatekeeping activity of Trump on Twitter indicates that he holds power over the general topics discussed, and it seems to be a strategic method to directly and indirectly pressure MSM outlets to change their tone and editorial stances.

Based on the empirical findings of this study and building on Herman and Chomsky's (1988) propaganda model, I argue that Twitter offers Trump a networked flak tool by which he attempts to continuously undermine the credibility of MSM and "discipline" it especially outlets that are most critical of his administration and policies (Reporters Without Borders, 2017). This networked flak activity is meant to undermine the general public's trust in MSM (Marwick & Lewis, 2017;Tavernise, 2016), especially enhanced by Trump's team during the 2016 election to suggest that mainstream "news cannot be trusted" (Faris et al., 2017, p. 21). This networked flaking strategy also exerts additional collective and possibly financial pressure on MSM in order to change its editorial stances. According to Lesley Stahl, the "60 Minutes" correspondent, Donald Trump revealed in July 2016 that he routinely attacks MSM to shield himself from negative coverage. When asked about his motives, Trump mentioned: "I do it to discredit you all and demean you all so when you write negative stories about me no one will believe you" (Schwarz, 2018). As part of his continuous efforts to undermine the credibility of MSM, Trump announced his "Fake News Awards" in January 2018 on his Twitter account, in which many outlets like the New York Times, ABC, CNN, the Washington Post, and Newsweek were targeted (CBC, 2018). According to the website (www.trumptwitterarc hive.com/) that archives all of Trump's tweets since he has become a president, Trump tweeted mostly on fake news (n ¼ 184) followed by Fox News or Sean Hannity (n ¼ 182). Finally and in a more recent development, Donald Trump repopularized the accusation that MSM is the "enemy of the people," that routinely practices a "Trump Derangement Syndrome," prompting the New York Times to protest against this label due to its potential harm to democracy and impact on the safety of journalists (Pengelly, 2018;Schwab, 2018). Indeed, Trump's Twitter attacks are networked due to the nature of the social media platform as he continues to practice a centralized gatekeeping activity, and the future will show the long-term impact of this strategy on MSM.

Finally, this study is limited in many aspects. Future studies can critically examine the news coverage changes of fake news in MSM throughout the previous decades since this study has provided a holistic overview of this coverage. Also, this study investigates the discourse on fake news on Twitter and the role of influentials rather than identifying all the original creators of fake news stories. Finally, the study uses topic modeling which is a computational method that has its own limitations in terms of decontextualizing the data set, so using some qualitative approaches can be useful in complementing its findings.
